title: The Repose of St. Boulos (Paul) of Thebes the First Hermit
narrative:
On the fifteenth day of Hamle, the Ethiopian Church solemnly commemorates the repose of Saint Paul of Thebes, venerated throughout the Oriental Christian world as the First Hermit — the man who preceded Anthony the Great in the desert life by several decades and whose existence Anthony himself confirmed when, guided by a vision, he made the long journey through the desert to find him. Paul had fled to the desert during the Decian persecution as a young man and had lived there for ninety years when Anthony found him — a man of over one hundred years, fed daily by a raven that brought him half a loaf of bread, clothed in a garment woven from palm leaves, knowing nothing of the world's affairs but everything of God.

The account of Anthony's visit to Paul, preserved in Jerome's Life of Paul and widely circulated in both Coptic and Ethiopic versions, is one of the great narratives of Christian ascetic literature. The two old men sat together in the palm-leaf cave, each insisting that the other should break the bread — neither willing to begin the meal without the other's blessing, each deferring to the holiness he recognised in the other. A raven brought a whole loaf that day rather than the usual half, and the two monks understood this as a gift from God for the occasion. When Paul died shortly afterward, Anthony saw his soul ascending to heaven in the company of Elijah and John the Baptist, and two lions came from the desert to dig his grave.

The Ethiopian Church's commemoration of Paul of Thebes on this day in Hamle is an occasion to meditate on the hidden dimension of the Church's life — that vast realm of solitary prayer that has always sustained the visible community of worship and mission. Paul spent ninety years alone in the desert, praying without ceasing, known to no one, visited by only one person during his entire adult life. Yet his life is commemorated annually, his intercession is sought, his example is held up as one of the highest forms of Christian life. The Ethiopian tradition, which has produced many solitary hermits in its own history, knows that the Church could not exist without them. May the intercession of Paul the First Hermit strengthen all who pray in hiddenness. Amen.
